Yosi Stahl

Mivara.io: A Post-mortem on an Early Venture

What I built, what I learned, and why stopping was the right decision.

The Vision: A Social Learning Ecosystem

The idea behind Mivara.io was to create a platform where anyone could learn from the practical, real-world knowledge of others. I envisioned a peer-to-peer ecosystem for skills, moving beyond traditional courses. Excited by the concept, I dove straight into building it.

Mivara

Connect. Learn. Share.

The Build: From Idea to a Functional Prototype

I spent several months designing and building a fully functional prototype from the ground up, using advanced no-code tools. This involved crafting the entire UI/UX and implementing the platform's core features.

The Lesson: Knowing Which Fights to Pick

There's a saying that great founders "make it work" through sheer force of will. But through the process of building, I came to a tougher realization: the even harder skill is knowing when not to force it. I had fallen in love with my solution without properly validating the problem. I had built a "nice-to-have," not a "must-have." And so, I made the difficult but necessary business decision to halt development, choosing to save my energy for a fight I knew I could win.

My Key Takeaway: Truly Understanding "The Lean Startup"

Mivara.io was one of the most valuable lessons of my entrepreneurial journey. It's what led me to not just read, but truly understand "The Lean Startup" methodology. It taught me that the most critical step isn't the build itself, but the validation of an idea. The "Build-Measure-Learn" feedback loop is now a core principle in how I approach every new venture.

"They say startups fail, but founders don't. This project was a failure. I am not."

On to the Next

This post-mortem serves as a constant reminder to always stay close to the user and the problem. Thank you for reading.
